transcript.text: Hey, definitely it was like...
This meeting is being recorded.
Get me out of this last call.
This is the one I'm looking forward to.
Love it.
Yeah, me too.
Me too.
No, I was fired up yesterday and got a positive reaction running things up the flagpole with the CFO.
That's awesome.
And I'll just...
Since I'm a marketer, I'll share with you what resonates.
Definitely.
When I opened with these guys work with Lovable and Ramp, that sort of did it, to be honest.
You know, I mean, both of them, but Lovable especially is just getting so much buzz around how they market and how they use content and how they use social that...
That's a pretty important case study for you guys.
case study That's
Yeah, we're definitely excited to have them as a partner and have a lot more names I can throw your way if it ever helps or if you needed a reference at one point, we can also do that.

But for today, I was thinking I could really just try to like peel back the curtain on our process as much as possible, show you what some of the deliverables look like, go into how a piece of content gets created.
And then from there, we can just talk a little bit more tactically about the scope that would be most impactful for the team at the team you're building at Elementum.
Yeah, cool.
Sounds great.
Let's do it.
So I want to start with talking about what happens super early on in the engagement.
And I mean like first two first two weeks.

And so what we do, right, is we try to develop the context engineering to make the workflows hyper specific to your brand, the personas that you're going after, and how your products and services are differentiated.
And so for any...
We've created all these business travel guides.
And one thing that we learned early on is that they wanted to create industry-specific content.
And so what we did was we set up this content OS is what we call it.
And we start with the science.
What is the keyword universe?
What is the search volume and the difficulty to rank against their top competitors?

And then from that, the resources we ingest and the kickoff call that we have with your team, we start to connect everything to topic clusters, which is where we're going to concentrate all the content that we create.
And so think of topic clusters, right, as where do you want authority?
Where do you want to be seen as the expert?
And so everything that we're going to do here and what we found with Engine was we want to go industry-specific is connected to those key personas that you're going after.
And then in the case of Engine, right, there's industry-specific content.
So we wanted to capture some nuance.
So
You'll also have a huge roadmap of the assignments that you can consider.
And we'll always give our opinion on what we should prioritize.
But what's nice is, right, it's all going to be starting with the keyword research and then connected to a topic cluster and then built specifically for the persona.
And the way that we shape the content is we actually create all these different artifacts.
And they're different with every client.
We have some standard ones, but they can be different for each workspace that I go into.
But essentially, this is getting into the mind of the ICP, right?
So for Engine, it's the construction operations manager.
And, you we've got this for every industry, the finance manager and then the project manager.
And this is essentially how is Engine differentiated and what are all of the pain points, jobs to be done, ways you win or lose their trust.
last one one So
Related to travel specifically and related to that role.
And so this is one of the artifacts that we use to shape the content that we create anytime we're doing something that's construction specific for engine.
And is this content actually ingested specifically into AI prompts or is this more guidance for your team?
This is ingested directly into the workflows.
And so you'll see some other artifacts here.
The standard ones are always going to be writing guidelines.
So what's the voice and tone, good and bad examples, ways to structure the content or talk about your competitors.
We don't need you to dictate this to us, but we'll calibrate around what we generate as a V1.
And we understand best practices from working with over 70 different clients and creating these different artifacts to inform the workflows that we build.
Yeah, got it.
And so if we move from here into so creating...
I'm going to show you one of the lovable examples here, mobile app development trends to watch in 2026.
What we do to create this piece of content is we set up a pipeline where we leverage those inputs and also instructions from our managing editors.

In addition to some specialized tools for deeper research, some perplexity, you know, can get the job done, but if you use something like equity, Alexa or Tavly, you're going to get a lot better fine-tuned research.
We can even point our research to specific sources.

And so what happens here is the research is going on for 30 minutes, and it's agentic so that if we don't hit a certain quality score, if there are too many gaps, it'll rerun the research pointed where those gaps are.
And you can see that the output here, right, is this massive research report that answers all
Several different questions with specific sources cited.
Goes on for quite a while.

And at the end of this is this massive bibliography of all of these very high, very credible, high quality sources, research firms and consulting, tech publications, financial publications, platform documentation, et cetera, et cetera.
And so what we've done is we've built all these workflows in code so that we can quickly adapt them as models change.
We can quickly customize them from client to client.
And if you inspect one of these workflows in the background, you'll see that there's a lot of different things that are happening.
It's generating a research plan of who is the target audience and what is the objective of the research that we're doing.
It's synthesizing the results and evaluating the quality.
It's storing the research so that we can fine tune the engine with the next.
It's
These we do that might be connected.
And then if you look at our workflows library, this gives a bit clearer look.
So if I go into the research specifically, or what was it called?
Sorry, research supervisor, that's right.
So you can see there's like hundreds of different workflows that we've built.
For the research supervisor, there's like a map of everything that's happening.
But then if you actually look at the activities, there are massive prompts that go into every single step that you're seeing more on the front end.
And then again, we use that context to inform how these prompts actually go in and execute.
Got it.
What is this platform that we're looking at?
This is like our own internal platform.
Okay, got it.
But it's more of like our back end, like admin that I don't think it's anything that's like client face.
But I'd like to show it just to kind of give you an idea of like what's actually happening in this research step.
Yeah, got it.
We move into drafting the article.
You can see that the research is used as an input.
Direction from the team is used as an input with artifacts.
The writing guidelines from the client is used as an input.
And then what I love is that each step builds on itself.
So we're not just using the writing guidelines as an input to draft the article.
We're taking the completely drafted article and then validating it against the writing guidelines so that we can be absolutely sure, right, it's written the way that you want it to be written.
We're doing the most relevant cross-linking to different content on your website.
We're adding source links.
And you saw from the research that there's a lot of credible sources that we can use in the way that we write the content.
And then a step that I...
Also really like, because AI is known to hallucinate, fact-checking.

So the deep research, right, all of the steps before this and steps within the steps that happen to actually get to this stage, what we do in the fact-checking step is we take the entire article, we extract passages, and then do more deep research to make sure that for any passage, we can actually validate against a confidence interval that the passage is accurate, right?

And so there's a good example here where there's a passage that says they rebuilt their website in less than one month versus the six months and $80,000 that traditional development would have required.
And there's some nuance to this, right?
It's, okay, that's a little bit too much of a blanket statement.
It costs six months and $80,000 for traditional development.
This is a timeline that Laura Stevenson says would have taken six months and $80,000 with traditional development.
And so it's capturing this in the fact-checking step.
And our team, you know, they have the ability to make changes throughout this process and be in the loop so that we're not just blindly, right, like creating a piece of content through AI.
They're able to go in and, you know, let's say I want to take this first paragraph.
So I'm going to just select this, add selection, add the personas artifacts.
And then let's just say I want to add three bullet points tailored to a female entrepreneur.
Probably not like a relevant example, but gives you an idea of how they can make like individual changes to the world, to the words.
But then they can also move faster by using the platform to actually leverage the context that we.
We have to shape the content based on, you know, their best practices as writers and SEO experts.
And so I really like this one because it actually brings a case study that we have on Lovable in that is a female entrepreneur building an app in the platform.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Super cool.
Any big questions you have?
I know it's, like, pretty confusing and, like, not easy to explain succinctly, so happy to be an open vote.
No, no, I totally get it.
I mean, I, yeah, I get the principles.
In one of the early screens when you were looking at keyword analysis, to what extent are you managing to, like, AEO, GEO, independent of SEO?
And, like, how should I think about the, like, keyword lists?
Yeah, for sure.
So when we think about AI search, we have some workflows that actually generate all the different prompts that seem to be the most relevant for you to track.

But we think about it in terms of if we can really understand everything that your ICP might be searching that's connected to your product in some way, and we generate really good artifacts around that.
As long as we create content that is connected to those topics, then we're going to be in a good place, especially if we index on keywords, right?
There's always going to be keywords.
There's just like a long tail with AI search where there's a lot more ways that someone can go in and prompt, right?
Maybe more detailed prompts that they can give.
And so we see a connection.
We see that as complementary SEO and AEO.
And if we can really understand the keyword universe and where you can win in traditional...
It's going to connect on the AI search side, as long as we're taking this audience-first approach of being the best answers to all the questions that they have.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Got it.
Got it.
Okay.
And then one more example I think would be helpful to show.
So Biologica, they are in like the women's supplement space.
And they just launched their site.
It was the former CEO of Allbirds and his wife.
And we've created all of the different – I think it's right here.
No, no, no.
Sorry.
It's – oh, yeah.
Sorry.
We've created all of these articles for them that are like very scientific and have like a very strict review process from their team.
Mm-hmm.
And so, you know, everything that you're seeing here, right, it's like reviewed by a medical advisor.
Bye.
Thank
These essentially like medical advice.
We stood up these custom pipelines for them where you can start to see it's a similar workflow, but there's a lot of additional steps.
There's one where we actually validate against their products.
There's another where we're going through a compliance review.
And so this just shows you how like we can really customize the workflows that we've built, but also like leverage the best practices from the other clients that we're working with.
And then you'll see with Biologica, they have all these different, all these unique artifacts.
So an ingredients dossier that's used in one of the steps in our workflow.
There's something, compliance rules, right?
That's part of that compliance step.
Yeah.
And so I think like, you know, if you try...
When it builds in a workflow orchestration tool, you have so far to go to get to the place where we're already at.

And that's why our approach, it's basically taking something that's working really well and taking a process that's rooted in the outcomes that you're trying to drive around search visibility and organic traffic.
And then basically understanding your audience so that we can tailor the workflows directly to, you know, the outcomes that you're trying to drive.
Yeah, got it.
Got it.
Okay.
Yeah.
Super helpful.
And so in that example, you've got people at Biologically in the loop on the content review process.
So they're actually getting an notification that says, hey, here's this new article.
Here's a section you need to go review, doing the work, marking off that it's done.
Yeah, we just send Google Docs over and basically have your team give feedback and approvals through like a shared Slack channel.
Okay, got it.
I think probably like three to six months into the partnership, you would begin to access what I'm showing you, which our team uses today.
Yeah.
And then within the next month, for sure, you'd have access to our AI visibility product.
Yeah, got it.
Got it.
Okay.
Okay.
So it's not, you know, you're working with an agency, right?
That's like doing everything on your behalf and may not be using technology much as part of the process.
It's, we use technology as a primary part of our process.
It allows us to move, to move much faster and get to higher qualities.
And we're also bringing our customers into the products that we're releasing without like additional cost or scope to the engagement.
It's just a benefit that you'd have of being connected to growthx.
Yeah.
Got it.
Got it.
Okay.
Can you drill down a little bit on what that initial four-week engagement would look like?
Yeah, absolutely.
Got a good resource for that.
So here we go.
So pre-kickoff, we'll take resources that you have around your brand, personas, positioning, product marketing.
We'll ingest those and we'll start to develop V1 of our artifacts.
We'll refine that after our kickoff call and our product deep dive to meetings that we have with your team to really get the nuance that isn't captured in the resources.
Week two, you've got the artifacts, you're reviewing those, you're helping us calibrate on the context that we're using, we're putting together.
That content OS that I showed you, and that was like an older version of like some of the content OSs that we're building out.
So it's, you know, it's improved, I think, from what you saw.
We're working together to make sure that the topics that we're going to create are relevant.
And we're starting to publish calibration articles by week four.
And then by week five and six, we're actually publishing five, sometimes even ten articles in your CMS.
And then week seven, that's when you're thinking about, do I want to continue with the 12-month phase two?
How do I feel about like the quality bar that GrowthX can create and the roadmap of assignments that they would work on?
And you get all these different deliverables as part of the engagement.
Customer market insights, competitive briefings, voice and style guide, editorial roadmap, and then calibration content.
But also, you know, what's not like explicitly said, but is done is...
Beyond calibration, we're publishing five to ten articles by the end of that eight-week engagement.
Yeah, got it.
Okay.
And have you thought more about, you know, we really don't have a lot.
And so how do we, you know, again, like, we don't have competitive battle cards.
We literally don't have a messaging house.
How do we, how do we go at that when we're really starting from stage zero and no product marketing?
Yeah.
What do you have?
Well, I mean.
On the resource side, like, like, what would you share with us that is created?
Yeah.
Yeah.
So, I mean, next week we're doing our sales kickoff.
As part of that, we're going to ship a new first call deck that, you know, obviously has some, you know,
Top-level messaging.
talks about our platform.
talks about some categories of use cases that we're using to try to identify the best place to land in a new customer.

I mean, we've got a bunch of decks that our sales team has been putting together, but it's literally, I mean, there's very few common assets, and it's a lot more AEs just doing their best to put together what they can to tell the story.
So we've got the content.
Are there any resources that articulate, like, why, you know, you have such a differentiated solution on the market that people are going to and dropping five tools as a result?
Like, that would probably be the most helpful, I think, is if we could capture that inside.
Yeah.
You know, we have some, right?
So there are some, but there's not a lot of depth.
Right.

So, like, one example that I think I mentioned is the fact that we run natively inside your data cloud, and that's an important differentiator for a bunch of reasons, whether it's compliance, whether it's security, whether that's no need to do integration work.
Um, so we've got that kind of messaging probably said in a lot of different ways, because it's authored by a lot of different people.
Um, but, but again, there's no, there's no product marketing team.
so there's no like definitive resource that explains that differentiator.
Any sort of like brand guidelines around how you want to sound or any sort of, uh, There's a brand, there's a brand guidelines document from like 2022 when they were all
Almost in a different business.
So yeah, I mean, that's a great example of something where I certainly have perspective.
And I think I'm aligned with the rest of the leadership teams.
But it's not just down anywhere.
No worries.
And then remind me, who are like the specific titles that you're going after?
Like personas, which folks do we ultimately want consuming the content we would create?
I mean, CIO and the CIO's directs, chief data officer and the chief data officer's directs, are really the two kind of biggest sets of audiences.
And then I would say that, you know, if you look at where we land, on the business side, it varies a fair amount by vertical.
But on the back office side, there are some very...
Common use cases in, like in procurement, in help desk, in IT, in finance, where certainly you're not going to roll out a solution without buying from, you know, from the business side.
Okay.

So, you know, often when we land, it is someone senior in IT saying, okay, I understand the vision, I understand the platform differentiators, and I know, and I know kind of long term how this can impact my business.
And then together, we try to identify, okay, what's the right use case to start?

And like one common example, just because it sits in IT, is software license management, you know, for big companies that don't have a fully automated way to track what SaaS licenses are being used by whom.
That's a great, that's a great example of a pretty well encapsulated use case.
Where you've got a clear business owner and you've got a huge amount of value.
And, you know, because I have two one-year-old dogs and they're, like, just borderline for being able to hang out in the office.
Um, um, you've got, so, so because, because it works so well, basically the, the, the sales motion is get buy-in on the overall vision, find the first use case.
And then the experience has been that once we land that first use case, the results kind of speak for themselves.
And then we tend to get a lot of pull from our customers around, okay, where can we go do more of this?
I think the biggest thing without a ton of resources is being able to have the trust established that we can publish without every piece being.
Scrutinized to a level that gets in the way to getting signals and driving visibility.
I think sometimes we'll set up a different section of the site if the team feels like the initial content that we're doing is too educational and not sophisticated enough.
But the reality is that you can get quick wins there.
So if we can have a streamlined review process to get to five articles per week, we don't need those resources to be effective.

We just need trust that what we do at the beginning is going to continue to get more sophisticated and going to give us some of those signals so that the pieces that may take a lot more time and come off as more thought leadership, they actually have a stronger impact because you've built more authority through the first few months of the content that we publish.
I feel pretty good that we would have the ability to move fast with you guys and not get hung up on every single article that we're writing.
Why are we writing it?
But I think if it was going to be a difficult review process, not having the resources plus that, it would probably make it a bad timing for the engagement.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, I'm not worried about the review process.
Honestly, in the foreseeable future, it's going to be me.
And then there will probably be some topics where I want to pull in somebody for product.
I mean, if we're shooting pretty lofty on the content, I may want our CEO or COO to take a look at some of this stuff.
But no, I mean, we're very much in a mode of, you know, perfection is the enemy of progress.
And we need to go really fast.
And again, we've got nothing.
mean, you've seen the stats on our site.
So, I mean, from what you said on the first call, right, it's like you have this massive opportunity to win anytime the right person is aware of what you're doing.

And I think that's what we would be extremely effective at is bringing you a ton of awareness that hopefully, you know, can fit the small window of the ICP that you're really going after, which is the company that would pay, you know, seven figures plus.
Yeah, exactly.
Yeah.

And I mean, one of the things that I do have in mind, too, is that because we have so little content to share, you know, I do want to make sure that we're putting out some stuff that our internal team is going to want to send a link to the customer that they're talking to or share it on LinkedIn.
And, you know, not that we need to have separate work streams to generate that content, but the go-to-market is starved for content.
And so, as long as we're not generating things that are.
Just noisy and, you know, very obviously don't have a lot of original content.
I think the uptake and the excitement is going to be really, really high.
Yeah, I mean, I think, you know, there's like a lot of education that's needed, right?
When you're thinking about deprecating legacy tools and spending a lot for a centralized solution.
I mean, we are using our own workflows to create content like this, which I've used in my sales outreach.
Like, which AI model should I choose?
And this goes like super deep into the model landscape, the pros and cons.
So I definitely think, you know, there's going to be a combination, right?
There's going to be some that is content that's easy to create at a really high quality.
That's going to get you immediate visibility.

And then there's others that are super detailed that capture the nuances of your product that we are able to weave in the further that we calibrate with you on, you know, what makes Elementum so special.
I know we're over time.
know we've been moving fast, but what can I do, I guess, to address any concerns you have or build confidence to move into more of a SoW review and conversation around picking off?
I mean, I would love to look at an SoW.
I'm talking to Brian Law this afternoon.
If I could say that I talked to somebody at Lovable or Ramp, I think that would go a really, really long way.
So if you've got somebody at either one of those companies that I can talk to, that'd be fantastic.
Yeah, for sure.
I think we do have a workshop with the head of marketing at Lovable, and they say a lot of good things about us.
Could we start with maybe the Brian Law conversation, the ROI document that I have, and the workshops that we have with these folks?
And if you still need us to, I mean, they're just super busy, I'm sure you know, but if you still need it, then I'll definitely make the ask.
Yeah.
Okay.
Hey, that sounds good.
Awesome.
I'm free anytime you need me.
Just let me know.
Okay, thanks, Tyler.
Appreciate it.
See you, Andrew.
Bye.
Yeah, we'll see you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Review GrowthX's AI content process and define a partnership scope for Elementum.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=72.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**AI-Powered Content OS:** GrowthX uses a proprietary platform to build a "Content OS." This system ingests client artifacts (personas, guidelines) and automates a multi-step workflow for high-volume, high-quality content production.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=128.0)
  - [**Rapid Ramp-Up:** A 4-week kickoff delivers a full content strategy and begins publishing 5–10 articles/week by week 5. This pace is critical for Elementum, which has no existing product marketing assets.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1022.0)
  - [**Critical Differentiator:** The AI workflow includes an agentic research phase (using tools like Equity, Tavly) and a rigorous fact-checking step to prevent hallucinations and ensure accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=308.0)
  - [**Path to SoW:** Andrew requested a Statement of Work (SoW) and a reference call with a client like Lovable or Ramp. Tyler will provide an ROI document, workshop recordings, and a reference call with Brian Law first.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1741.0)

## Topics

### Elementum's Content Gap & Needs

  - [**Problem:** Elementum has no formal product marketing assets (messaging, battle cards, personas), relying on ad-hoc sales decks.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1148.0)
  - [**Target Audience:** CIOs/CDOs and their directs, plus business owners in procurement, help desk, IT, and finance.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1338.0)
  - [**Sales Motion:** Secure buy-in on the platform vision → land an initial use case (e.g., software license management) → expand from there.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1396.0)
  - [**Goal:** Produce high-quality content that the go-to-market team can immediately use and share.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1645.0)

### GrowthX's AI Content OS

  - [**Foundation:** A "Content OS" built on keyword research and topic clusters to establish authority.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=128.0)
  - [**Inputs:** Client artifacts (personas, writing guidelines, product differentiators) are ingested directly into the AI workflow.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=253.0)
  - [**Workflow Pipeline:**](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=308.0)
    1.  [**Agentic Research:** Uses specialized tools (Equity, Tavly) for deep, cited research. The process reruns if quality scores aren't met.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=308.0)
    2.  [**Drafting:** Uses research and client artifacts to generate the article.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=488.0)
    3.  [**Validation:** The draft is checked against writing guidelines and optimized with internal/external links.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=503.0)
    4.  [**Fact-Checking:** Passages are extracted and re-researched to validate accuracy against a confidence interval, correcting any AI hallucinations.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [**Human Oversight:** Editors can intervene at any stage to guide the AI, ensuring quality and strategic alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=605.0)

### Partnership Scope & Timeline

  - [**Initial 4-Week Engagement:**](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1022.0)
      - [**Week 1:** Ingest Elementum resources; kickoff calls to refine V1 artifacts.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1022.0)
      - [**Week 2:** Finalize artifacts and the "Content OS."](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1070.0)
      - [**Week 4:** Publish initial "calibration articles."](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1098.0)
  - [**Post-Kickoff:**](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1098.0)
      - [**Weeks 5–6:** Publish 5–10 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1098.0)
      - [**Week 7:** Review progress and decide on a 12-month "Phase 2" engagement.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=1109.0)
  - [**Client Access:**](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
      - [**Month 1:** Access to the "AI visibility product."](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
      - [**Months 3–6:** Potential access to GrowthX's internal platform.](https://fathom.video/share/jxSgRFJH4FzBQjbRJD5PhqDU13zExRLs?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
